summaries.Geology: Surtsey is a volcanic island located approximately 32 km south of the Icelandic coast, formed by volcanic eruptions occurring between 1963 and 1967, representing the canonical example of shallow-to-emergent phreatomagmatic eruptions. Whole-rock and sideromelane glass analyses of tephra from time- and space-located samples throughout Surtsey's eruption define a Surtsey Magma Series and Surtsey Melt Series, with progressive geochemical changes in eruptive products explained by mixing between ephemeral and poorly connected volumes of broadly EVZ-like alkalic basalt typical of Iceland's Vestmannaeyjar and primitive ridge-like basalt formed at the propagating ridge tip. Surtsey exemplifies processes occurring at the extreme propagating tip of an off-axis ridge and demonstrated the dynamics of magma-water interaction in subaqueous eruptions. In 1979, a joint effort by the Icelandic Museum of Natural History and the Geothermal Research program of the US Geological Survey drilled a 180.6 metre-deep borehole into Surtsey, achieving very good core recovery, with the Surtsey magma series including major-element analyses of sideromelane glass in tephras extracted from the core spanning 180.1 to 0.1 m depth, interpreted to represent the pre-emergent growth of the island in the lead up to the establishment of the emergent Surtur I vent.
summaries.Recent development: A recent study published in 2024 examined drill cores from Surtsey using the Advanced Light Source (ALS) to gain unique insights into the transformation of volcanic glass to form mineral cements in the basaltic rock of underwater volcanoes, with the research detailing material and mechanical properties of young basalt in drill cores from the oceanic island. The study, authored by M.D. Jackson, M.J. Heap, G. Vola, M. Ardit, J.M. Rhodes, J.G. Peterson, N. Tamura, and M.T. Gudmundsson, was published in the Geological Society of America Bulletin in early 2024 under doi:10.1130/B37037.1. Marie Jackson, a research professor of geology and geophysics, noted that Surtsey is significant because it offers a rare opportunity to observe geologic processes at the human scale and serves as a window into the oceanic crust. The drill cores revealed the presence of Al-tobermorite embedded in a fine-ash matrix, providing new understanding of mineral cementation processes in basaltic oceanic island formations. Surtsey has held UNESCO World Heritage Site status since 2008 and continues to function as a protected natural laboratory for both biological and geological research.
summaries.Biology: Surtsey was born as a new volcanic island in 1963-67 and has since played a major role in studies of succession and colonisation, serving as one of the few long-term study sites worldwide on primary succession and providing a unique scientific record of the colonisation of land by plants, animals, and marine organisms. The island has been legally protected from its birth, functioning as a pristine natural laboratory free from human interference. The most widespread plant species on Surtsey include sea sandwort, procumbent pearlwort, common mouse ear, annual meadow-grass Poa annua, and lyme grass, while the forb-rich grassland is dominated by smooth meadow-grass Poa trivialis, arctic fescue Festuca rubra, and lyme grass, with forbs including mouse-ear Cerastium alpinum, sea mayweed Tripleurospermum maritimum, common chickweed Stellaria media, scurvygrass, and sea sandwort. Bryophyte diversity is relatively high, with a significant portion of the community covered by bryophytes, and this community is beginning to resemble bird-enriched grasslands elsewhere in the archipelago. Vascular-plant-dominated vegetation now covers an estimated 1–2% of the island's surface, aided by winds and birds bringing in disseminules, with a total of 25 species of vascular plants recorded despite a general shortage of water as rain seeps rapidly into the surface lava, sand, or tuff. Research on Surtsey highlights how birds played a critical role in bringing life to the island, challenging earlier assumptions that plant colonisation of remote islands depended primarily on long-distance dispersal traits, and instead emphasising the importance of real biological interactions in ecological models. Long-term research on Surtsey continues to provide invaluable data on biological colonisation, ecosystem development, and adaptation, offering insight into how life establishes itself, evolves, and reacts to a changing world.
summaries.Human impact: Surtsey has been legally protected from its birth in 1963, with human impact strictly minimized through prohibition of going ashore, diving by the island, disturbing natural features, introducing organisms, minerals and soils, or leaving waste on the island, with nearby construction also strictly controlled. The island is visited by a single group of approximately 10 scientists in a normal year, who carry out low-impact ecological succession monitoring and general status studies of wildlife and flora annually. The purpose of strictly prohibiting visits is to ensure that colonisation by plants and animals, biotic succession, and the shaping of geological formations will be as natural as possible and that human disruption will be minimised. The island was formally protected in 1965, limiting human impact to only a few visits annually by scientists, and it is managed in conformity with the requirements of an IUCN category 1a Strict Nature Reserve. The ongoing ecological processes have remained unaffected by human activities, making Surtsey a unique natural laboratory of global significance, and the most significant management issue going forward is to retain the level of control and protection from human influence that has characterised its protective history, while also recognising the potential for human disturbance inherent in an island ecosystem.
